title: BBC News English
description: Improve your English with real BBC news stories — expand vocabulary and comprehension

episodes.title: The Global Economy in 2024
episodes.description: Understanding economic trends and how they affect everyday life

transcript.text: This is BBC World News. I'm your presenter, and today we look at the global economic outlook for 2024.
After years of turbulence caused by the pandemic and geopolitical tensions, economists are cautiously hopeful.
Turbulence means a state of confusion and disorder — in economics, it refers to instability in markets.
Inflation — the rate at which prices rise — has been the dominant concern for central banks worldwide.
In the UK and US, inflation peaked at over 10% in 2022 but has since fallen significantly.
Central banks raised interest rates aggressively to bring inflation under control.
Interest rates are the cost of borrowing money. Higher rates slow spending and reduce inflation.
Now, with inflation easing, there are questions about when rate cuts might begin.
Easing means becoming less severe or intense — so inflation easing means prices are rising more slowly.
The International Monetary Fund projects global growth of around 3% — below the historical average but avoiding recession.
A recession is officially defined as two consecutive quarters of negative economic growth.
Emerging markets like India and Vietnam are outpacing developed economies in growth terms.
Outpacing means growing faster than something else.
Our economics correspondent reports on what this means for ordinary workers and consumers.
Key vocabulary today: turbulence, inflation, interest rates, recession, emerging markets, outpacing.

episodes.title: Technology and Democracy
episodes.description: How social media and AI are reshaping politics and public debate

transcript.text: The relationship between technology and democracy is under scrutiny like never before.
Scrutiny means close and critical examination — when something is under scrutiny, it is being watched carefully.
Social media platforms have been accused of amplifying misinformation and polarizing societies.
Misinformation is false or inaccurate information. Polarizing means creating strong division between groups.
Governments around the world are grappling with how to regulate these platforms.
Grappling means struggling to deal with something difficult.
The EU's Digital Services Act is one of the most significant regulatory frameworks to date.
It requires large platforms to assess and mitigate systemic risks — including threats to elections.
Mitigate means to reduce the severity of something.
Meanwhile, artificial intelligence tools are now being used to generate deepfakes — convincing but false videos of public figures.
Experts warn that this could undermine trust in legitimate news sources.
Undermine means to erode or weaken something gradually.
Some researchers argue for digital literacy programs in schools — teaching young people to evaluate sources critically.
Digital literacy is the ability to use and evaluate digital technology and online content responsibly.
Key terms today: scrutiny, misinformation, polarizing, grappling, mitigate, undermine, digital literacy.

episodes.title: Climate Action and Green Energy
episodes.description: The latest developments in the global shift toward renewable energy

transcript.text: Renewable energy now accounts for over 30% of global electricity generation — a record high.
Accounts for means represents or makes up a certain proportion of a total.
Solar and wind power have seen dramatic cost reductions over the past decade.
Dramatic here means very significant or striking — not theatrical.
In 2010, solar energy was ten times more expensive than it is today.
This has made renewables competitive with — and often cheaper than — fossil fuels.
Fossil fuels include coal, oil, and natural gas — energy sources formed from ancient organic material.
However, challenges remain. Grids must be upgraded to handle the intermittent nature of renewables.
Intermittent means not continuous — solar panels only generate power when the sun shines.
Battery storage technology is advancing rapidly to address this problem.
Countries like Denmark now generate over 50% of electricity from wind on an average day.
The UK aims to decarbonize its electricity grid entirely by 2030.
Decarbonize means to eliminate carbon dioxide emissions — usually by replacing fossil fuels with clean energy.
Key vocabulary: accounts for, dramatic, fossil fuels, intermittent, battery storage, decarbonize.
